About

"The Atlas speaks directly in a method of its own choosing. All translation of its words is considered blasphemy."

The Atlassian Bigram Index is an interactive tool built for No Man's Sky. It was built on the foundations of the Glyph Rosetta Dataset, a complete lexical record of the five alien languages, and their associated glyphs. The dataset was compiled against Update 6.33.

There are 16 glyphs in No Man's Sky that are used for portal travel. Every word across all five alien languages maps to its assigned glyph via its first two letters, without exception across 4,820 documented words. Because of this, even words not included in the game's five languages can have a glyph based on their first two letters.

Hello Games has essentially built a universal bigram index. This tool simply applies their rules to all words.

Type any word or phrase you can think of. The tool returns the glyph assignments based on the first two letters of each word. A secondary hex output allows users to copy-paste the address as needed.

Details on how the entire system works can be found in the Science section of this site.

Published works

  • The Atlas Code: A Hitchhiker's Guide to No Man's Sky
  • The Missing Addendum v1.1 (Glyph Rosetta Dataset)
  • The Atlas Code: Errata

Tool created by N. Sand and D. Mowatt.

Science

The Bigram Law

The game No Man's Sky contains five learnable alien languages. Every word across all five alien languages (Atlas, Autophage, Gek, Korvax, and Vy'keen) maps to one of the game's 16 glyphs via its first two letters. These two letters are called a 'bigram'. Every one of these bigrams is assigned to only one glyph. This principle holds without exception across 4,820 documented words. These mappings can be seen in the game's language menus. These mappings have been compiled into a dataset and verified across multiple independent verification passes.

Missing bigrams are rare. Hello Games created enough bigrams to cover most English word openings. If you encounter one, note the bigram and consider reaching out to Hello Games directly — they are the ones who can add it to the system.

Researchers are encouraged to check the Glyph Rosetta Dataset for themselves, compiled in The Missing Addendum v1.1 and linked in Further Reading below.

Example

Note: These examples are also demonstrated visually in the How to Use section, showing how matched and missing bigrams appear in the index.

All RE words across all five languages map to Glyph 1. For this example we will use 6 words. The words reality, remain, respect, restitution, return, and reward all begin with the bigram RE, so you will see Glyph 1 assigned to these words in the in-game menus. RE is one of the 12 bigrams assigned to that glyph. The following are in-game menu visuals confirming this mapping:

Missing Bigram Example

The words Utopia, utilize, and utensil all begin with the bigram UT. UT has no assigned glyph. There are no UT words represented across any of the five alien languages in No Man's Sky, so no in-game visual example exists for this bigram.

The Complete Bigram Reference

All 16 glyphs and their assigned bigrams. The full reference, alongside visual glyph cards, can also be found in the Bigrams section.

How Many Bigrams Are There?

The dataset reveals 185 unique two-letter combinations covering the great majority of word openings in the English language. Each of the game's 16 glyphs accounts for a range of bigrams, from a low of 7 to a maximum of 17.

The breadth of this system is a credit to Hello Games. With 185 bigrams covering most English word openings, the system functions as a universal index. What this means is words that do not appear anywhere in the game can still be mapped to a glyph. Built on the patterns of the English language, it extends well beyond it, likely covering significant portions of other languages as well. It is a brilliantly constructed foundation, which is precisely why this tool exists: to find the glyph of any word you can think of.

Oddities and Single Letters

There are three odd words that have bigrams, but are irregular in form: 'hmmm', 'zzzzz', and '9a'. All three map to glyphs and have been included.

Furthermore, despite not being bigrams, the words 'I' and 'A' are represented by glyphs 1 and 9 respectively.

Bigram Verification

Anyone with access to No Man's Sky can verify these mappings directly. The game's language menus display every learnable word alongside its assigned glyph. Open any language menu and the bigram law is visible immediately.

For those who do not own the game or want a deeper look at the data, the complete Glyph Rosetta Dataset is available on Zenodo: The Missing Addendum v1.1. It includes Excel worksheets, all in-game menu screenshots, and a cataloguing system for rapid cross-referencing.

The Formula Anomaly

The word 'formula' violates the bigram law. It appears in both Gek and Korvax, and in both languages it points consistently to Glyph 1 rather than its expected assignment of Glyph 4, where all other FO words fall. Since FO has its own full representation on Glyph 4, the word 'formula' is classified as an anomaly rather than an exception to the rule. This anomaly is consistent, reproducible, and cross-referenceable using the dataset's cataloguing system.

The intentionality of this anomaly is questionable. During the Relics Update 5.6, Hello Games did a full pass of all five languages, fixing internal string errors and adding new words. Either they missed the formula anomaly during that pass, or it is an intentional part of their bigram system. This only leads to further questions about the mystery of their entire language system.

This tool takes the anomaly into account with a swap option, outlined in the How to Use section.

The Autophage Ciphertext

Twelve anomalous words exist within the Autophage language. In order of appearance within the in-game language menus:

  • hell0
  • 0wn
  • prebu1lt-entitψ
  • stαff
  • Korvαx
  • erαsure
  • prebu1lt
  • deςerves
  • c0nςtruct
  • inv1tation
  • n0n-convεrgent
  • At1αs

These words use binary digits and Greek characters to replace similar-looking Latin letters. When the substituted characters are extracted in order of appearance, they yield exactly 8 binary digits and 8 Greek letters, a 16-character sequence:

001ψααα1ς0ς10ε1α

Whether this is intentional design or coincidence remains an open question.

Numbers from The Atlas Code

The Atlas Code documents a verifiable, albeit speculative, transmedia narrative that Hello Games has layered throughout their game and surrounding media. The bigram system revealed many of the numbers already covered in the original manuscript that preceded the Glyph Rosetta Dataset.

  • 7 is found on Exotic Ships, the Starter Pistol, and Planetary Archives.
  • 17 is associated with the Void Mother.
  • 19, the whisper of the 19th minute.
  • 12 is the number of the Pathfinder Update 1.2.
  • 23 is ψ, the 23rd letter of the Greek alphabet and the scientific symbol for a wavefunction. The Starter Pistol is called the Waveform Focuser N56-P — only one instance of many uses of waveform language throughout the game.
  • 31 is the exact number of Boundary Failure logs documenting the Atlas's descent into rampancy.
  • 42 is the answer to life, the universe, and everything, from Douglas Adams' A Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy.

Click any term below to load it into the index:

  • I and A are represented by glyphs 1 and 9 respectively. Together, 19.
  • Hello World: hex 17
  • Hello Games: hex 12, portal numbers 23
  • The Atlas: hex 31, portal numbers 42

Even the bigrams range from 7 to 17. This kind of numerical intentionality permeates the bigram law itself. This hidden numerical code, and the full transmedia narrative surrounding it, has been explored in depth in The Atlas Code: A Hitchhiker's Guide to No Man's Sky, linked in the Further Reading section below.

Further Reading

The transmedia studies and the Glyph Rosetta Dataset, along with its Errata, are all publicly available on Zenodo.

  • The Atlas Code: A Hitchhiker's Guide to No Man's Sky — a transmedia analysis documenting the numerical framework, lore structure, and the discovery of the bigram law
  • The Missing Addendum v1.1 — the complete Glyph Rosetta Dataset, version locked to No Man's Sky Update 6.33
  • The Atlas Code: Errata — formal corrections to both publications, with a complete changelog for every change made between versions

How to Use

Accessibility Mode

Accessibility Mode converts the entire site to monochrome for colour blind users or those who prefer reduced colour.

Accessibility

There is also a toggle beside the Word Lookup and Reverse Lookup tabs on the main page. Both toggles work independently.

Basic use

Type any word or phrase into the input field. The glyphs and hex output update live as you type. Words with bigrams that have no assigned glyphs show a red X in the glyph slot, and a white question mark in the hex output.

Bigrams that do not have assigned glyphs are the responsibility of Hello Games. It will be up to them if they want to add more bigrams to the game in the future.

Word Examples

The following examples are referenced from the Science section and show what a matched and missing bigram look like in practice.

Example

The words return, reward, and real all begin with the bigram RE. They will always map to RE's assigned glyph, which is Glyph 1. This rule holds for every single learnable word in No Man's Sky that begins with RE, and any other bigrams that Hello Games included.

REturn
REward
REal
bigram RE
Glyph 1

Missing Bigram Example

The words Utopia, utilize, and utensil all begin with the bigram UT. UT is not assigned a glyph in Hello Games' bigram system. They will show a red X in the glyph slot and a white question mark in the hex output.

UTopia
UTilize
UTensil
bigram UT
✕
no match

The Portal System

The 16 glyphs in this tool correspond directly to the portal glyphs used in No Man's Sky. Only 12 are needed for a valid portal address for portal travel. The hex output is a shorthand representation of those same glyphs, used in portal decoders such as nmsportals.github.io to verify addresses or share locations out of game. If you are unfamiliar with the portal system or need a refresher, the No Man's Sky Portal Address wiki has everything you need.

Hex output colours

The hex output colour-codes are based on how many glyph-matched words are in the input field, allowing researchers to quickly see their match count at a glance. Words with no assigned glyph show a white question mark in the hex output.

A perfect 12 matched words shows entirely in blue. Any input under 12 matched words will show as green. Any amount of words over 12 shows the first 12 in green and the remainder in red.

Note: colour coding is not represented in Accessibility Mode. Glyph states are indicated by shades of grey in its place.

Hex Output Colour Examples

Under 12 — Green
0123456789A
Exactly 12 — Blue
0123456789AB
Over 12 — First 12 Green, Remainder Red
0123456789ABCDEF
Missing Bigram — White ?
012345678?AB

This intuitive design is to assist researchers in quickly copy-pasting 12 hex characters even if they go over, or to show them clearly when they are under 12 words.

Glyph to Hex Conversion Table

During testing, some users found the differences between glyph numbers and their hex equivalents confusing. The Glyph to Hex Conversion Table button beside the hex output field opens a quick-reference table to resolve this at a glance.

Word limit

The tool accepts up to 16 words. The word counter in the input field shows your current count. More words can be added but any words over the 16 count will not show glyph assignments.

FO toggle

Two bigrams share the letters FO. This has been called the formula anomaly, because the word 'formula' maps to Glyph 1 in both Korvax and Gek, while all other FO words are assigned to Glyph 4.

When a word beginning with FO is input, the glyph slot defaults to Glyph 4 with a distinct purple border. Clicking the purple slot toggles the glyph to Glyph 1. The slot turns magenta when the anomaly glyph is active.

Default — Glyph 4
Purple border
⇄ G:1
Formula Anomaly — Glyph 1
Magenta border
⇄ G:4

In Accessibility Mode, the formula anomaly glyph appears brighter than the default. Click either slot to toggle between the two. The ⇄ indicator below shows the glyph you can switch to.

You can toggle back and forth at will.

Click here to test it out FOR yourself

Reset and Clear

  • Reset: saves your current phrase to history and returns to Hello World
  • Clear: saves your current phrase to history and wipes the field

Recent History

Your last five phrases are saved below the input field. Hitting Enter, Reset, or Clear will save the current phrase to history. Click any recent phrase to reload it instantly. The small X beside Recent History will prompt you to clear your history. A confirmation dialogue will ask before doing so.

Reverse Lookup

The Reverse Lookup tab accepts a hex portal address of up to 12 characters. Enter any address to reveal its corresponding glyphs. Hovering over each glyph displays all of its assigned bigrams. Use these bigrams to build your own phrase that produces the same address, creating a personalised key to any location in the game.

The Bigrams

All 16 glyphs and their assigned bigrams. Every learnable word in No Man's Sky's five languages begins with one of these letter pairs. Every letter pair maps to one of the 16 Glyphs, which are used exclusively as addresses for Portal travel.

The Bigram Law is the law that governs the entire language system.

The Library of Babel

"The universe (which others call the Library) is composed of an indefinite and perhaps infinite number of hexagonal galleries."

"They were urged on by the delirium of trying to reach the books in the Crimson Hexagon: books whose format is smaller than usual, all-powerful, illustrated and magical."

Jorge Luis Borges  ·  The Library of Babel  ·  1941

The Theory

Did Hello Games build Borges' Library of Babel? Can the stars be mapped by word alone? No Man's Sky is composed of an indefinite and perhaps infinite number of star systems, each reachable through portal glyphs. Each portal glyph can be mapped to a word. All hidden within a Crimson Hexagon, also known as the Atlas. This language system is smaller than usual, illustrated in the language menus, and magical once understood.

Hello Games didn't have to build this. The game is extraordinary without it. But they built a complete, consistent, elegant language system across five alien civilisations, hid it in plain sight inside their menus, and said nothing. They built it out of love for a community that might never find it.

Implications

The bigram system extends far beyond the 4,820 words in the game. With 185 bigrams covering most English word openings, most words you can think of will map to a glyph. Most 12-word sequences will generate a portal address. Not all will be valid. Some return corrupted locations, others do not.

The first confirmed valid portal address was found using the first word of each of the first 12 lines of Shakespeare's Sonnet 130. That method is only one approach. Consider the first 12 words of your favourite text. Every first word of the first 12 chapters of your favourite book. Your favourite line of your favourite song. The opening line of Genesis 11. The system is consistent and the only limits are those of your own imagination.

Use the Reverse Lookup tab to find the bigrams for any address and build your own phrase to reach it. Create passphrases that lead to secret systems. Hidden meeting points accessible only to those who know the source text. Clandestine coordinates for in-game civilisations. The amount of possible addresses is, like the game itself, effectively without limit.

Examples — Confirmed Portal Addresses

The following phrase has been tested and confirmed as a valid portal address in No Man's Sky. A communications station has been placed at this location. This is the first time a literary portal address has been documented in No Man's Sky history.

This table converts between in-game glyph numbers and their hexadecimal equivalents. Hexadecimal outputs can be used with portal decoders such as nmsportals.github.io to check their validity.

Hex Output
1. Enter any word or phrase to see its assigned glyphs.
2. Words without an assigned bigram show a red X in the glyph section and a white question mark in the hex output.
1. Enter your desired address into the hex field to reveal the glyphs.
2. Hover over the glyphs to see their assigned bigrams.
3. Create your own phrase using those bigrams to reach your system.
